summary: I was born in Karen State, Burma, where my early life was shaped by the ongoing civil war. My father was killed by Burmese soldiers there in 1998, which deeply affected my family. Growing up in Karen State was difficult, especially after losing my father, and it forced me to seek refuge elsewhere for safety and education.

Burma is my homeland, but it has never been stable due to the revolution between the government and ethnic groups like the Karen. I lived there until my father was killed by Burmese soldiers in 1998, which marked a turning point in my life. Because of the ongoing conflict and lack of opportunities, I eventually had to leave Burma to find a better future.

Burmese soldiers played a tragic role in my life when they killed my father in the forest in 1998. My father was an innocent villager, and his death was a painful reminder of the conflict in Burma. This event deeply impacted me, shaping my understanding of the struggles faced by my people.

After leaving Burma, I spent thirteen years living in refugee camps along the Thai-Burma border. It was there that I learned valuable skills like filming and editing video, and I sought education despite the hardships. The refugee camps were only temporary, and I always hoped for a stable place where I could build a peaceful future.

I immigrated to the United States in September 2014 to build a new life filled with peace and freedom. Here, I have learned that education, money, and time are powerful resources that can help me succeed. Although life in the U.S. is different from Burma, I remain connected to my Karen roots and the ongoing struggle for peace.

I arrived in St. Paul, Minnesota in September 2014 as part of my journey to find stability and opportunity. This city became my new home where I started to set new hopes for my future. Living in St. Paul has given me a chance to pursue education and a better life, even as I continue to remember and support my Karen people.
